questions.question: What is system design?
questions.answer: System design is the process of defining architecture, components, modules, interfaces, and data flow to satisfy specified functional and non-functional requirements.

questions.question: What is the difference between monolithic and microservices architecture?
questions.answer: Monolithic: single deployable unit with all modules. Microservices: suite of small, independent services each with own database, communicating via APIs.

questions.question: What is horizontal vs vertical scaling?
questions.answer: Horizontal scaling adds more machines (scale out). Vertical scaling adds more power (CPU/RAM) to existing machine (scale up).

questions.question: What is load balancing?
questions.answer: Distributing incoming network traffic across multiple backend servers using algorithms (round robin, least connections, IP hash, consistent hashing).

questions.question: What are common load balancing algorithms?
questions.answer: Round robin, least connections, least response time, IP hash, consistent hashing, weighted round robin.

questions.question: What is a reverse proxy?
questions.answer: Server that sits in front of backend servers, forwarding client requests, providing load balancing, caching, SSL termination, and security.

questions.question: What is the difference between forward proxy and reverse proxy?
questions.answer: Forward proxy hides clients (corporate filter, anonymity). Reverse proxy hides servers (load balancer, CDN, API gateway).

questions.question: What is caching? Why is it used?
questions.answer: Storing frequently accessed data in fast temporary storage (memory) to reduce latency, decrease database load, and improve throughput.

questions.question: What are caching strategies (cache aside, read-through, write-through, write-behind)?
questions.answer: Cache aside: app manages cache. Read-through: cache loads missing data. Write-through: writes to cache and DB sync. Write-behind: writes to cache, async to DB.

questions.question: What is cache eviction policy? Name common ones.
questions.answer: LRU (Least Recently Used), LFU (Least Frequently Used), FIFO, TTL (Time To Live), random, or MRU (Most Recently Used).

questions.question: What is a CDN (Content Delivery Network)?
questions.answer: Distributed edge servers caching static assets (images, CSS, videos) close to users, reducing latency and origin load.

questions.question: What is database indexing?
questions.answer: Data structure (B-tree, hash) improving query speed at cost of slower writes and extra storage.

questions.question: What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases?
questions.answer: SQL: relational, ACID, structured schema, vertical scaling (MySQL, PostgreSQL). NoSQL: non-relational, flexible schema, horizontal scaling (MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis).

questions.question: What are types of NoSQL databases?
questions.answer: Document (MongoDB), Key-Value (Redis, DynamoDB), Column-family (Cassandra, HBase), Graph (Neo4j).

questions.question: What is database replication?
questions.answer: Copying data from master to replica servers, providing high availability, read scalability, and disaster recovery.

questions.question: What is master-slave vs master-master replication?
questions.answer: Master-slave: one master writes, slaves read. Master-master: multiple masters accept writes, conflict resolution needed.

questions.question: What is database sharding?
questions.answer: Horizontal partitioning of data across multiple databases (shards) by shard key (user_id, region), enabling horizontal scaling.

questions.question: What are common sharding strategies?
questions.answer: Range-based (user 1-1000 on shard A), hash-based (consistent hashing), directory-based (lookup table).

questions.question: What is CAP theorem?
questions.answer: Distributed systems can only achieve two of three: Consistency (same data everywhere), Availability (always responds), Partition tolerance (works despite network failures).

questions.question: What is PACELC theorem?
questions.answer: Extension of CAP: even without partition (P), system trades Latency (L) vs Consistency (C).

questions.question: What is consistency in distributed systems?
questions.answer: All nodes see same data at same time. Strong consistency (linearizability), eventual consistency (eventually converge).

questions.question: What is eventual consistency?
questions.answer: Data will become consistent over time if no new updates; used for high availability (DNS, DynamoDB).

questions.question: What is the difference between stateful and stateless services?
questions.answer: Stateful service stores client session data (e.g., shopping cart). Stateless service has no client context; any instance works, easier to scale.

questions.question: What is a distributed transaction? What is 2PC?
questions.answer: Transaction across multiple databases. Two-Phase Commit (Prepare, Commit) ensures atomicity but slow, blocking.

questions.question: What is the Saga pattern?
questions.answer: Distributed transaction alternative using sequence of local transactions with compensating transactions (rollback).

questions.question: What is idempotency in system design?
questions.answer: Operation producing same result on multiple invocations (e.g., PUT /orders/123). Critical for retries in distributed systems.

questions.question: What is message queue? Examples.
questions.answer: Asynchronous communication buffer where producer sends messages, consumer pulls. Examples: RabbitMQ, Apache Kafka, Amazon SQS.

questions.question: What is pub/sub (publish-subscribe) pattern?
questions.answer: Producers (publishers) send messages to topics; multiple consumers (subscribers) receive copies. Decouples senders and receivers.

questions.question: What is Kafka? When would you use it?
questions.answer: Distributed event streaming platform with persistent logs, high throughput, replayability. Used for log aggregation, metrics, CDC, real-time pipelines.

questions.question: What is the difference between Kafka and RabbitMQ?
questions.answer: Kafka: persistent log, replay, high throughput, ordered per partition. RabbitMQ: smart broker, complex routing, transient messages, lower latency.

questions.question: What is an API gateway? What does it do?
questions.answer: Single entry point for microservices handling authentication, rate limiting, request routing, aggregation, caching, and logging.

questions.question: What is service discovery?
questions.answer: Mechanism for services to find each other's network locations. Client-side (Eureka) or server-side (Consul, Kubernetes DNS).

questions.question: What is a circuit breaker pattern?
questions.answer: Stops calls to failing service after threshold, opens circuit, fails fast, and periodically tests recovery, preventing cascading failures.

questions.question: What is a bulkhead pattern?
questions.answer: Isolates failures by partitioning resources (thread pools, connection pools) so one service's failure doesn't exhaust all resources.

questions.question: What is retry with exponential backoff?
questions.answer: Retrying failed requests with exponentially increasing delays (100ms, 200ms, 400ms) + random jitter to avoid thundering herd.

questions.question: What is a dead letter queue (DLQ)?
questions.answer: Queue for messages that failed processing after retries, allowing analysis, replay, or manual intervention.

questions.question: What is rate limiting? How implemented?
questions.answer: Restricting request rates per client. Algorithms: token bucket, leaky bucket, sliding window. Implemented at API gateway or middleware.

questions.question: What is a bloom filter? Use case?
questions.answer: Probabilistic data structure testing set membership (false positives, no false negatives). Used to avoid cache penetration (non-existent keys).

questions.question: What is consistent hashing? Why use it?
questions.answer: Hashing technique minimizing remapping when hash table resizes. Used in caching (memcached), sharding (DynamoDB), and load balancing.

questions.question: What is a distributed cache? Example?
questions.answer: Cache spread across multiple nodes (Redis Cluster, Memcached). Provides high capacity, fault tolerance, horizontal scaling.

questions.question: What is a CDN cache invalidation?
questions.answer: Removing stale content from edge nodes after origin update using purge (immediate), TTL expiration, or versioned URLs (cache-busting).

questions.question: What is database connection pooling?
questions.answer: Reusing pre-opened database connections to reduce overhead of establishing new connections per request.

questions.question: What is leader election in distributed systems? Examples.
questions.answer: Algorithms (Bully, Raft) electing single coordinator to prevent split-brain. Used in ZooKeeper, etcd, Kafka controller.

questions.question: What is the Raft consensus algorithm?
questions.answer: Consensus protocol with leader election, log replication, and safety. More understandable than Paxos; used in etcd, CockroachDB, TiKV.

questions.question: What is a distributed lock? Implementation?
questions.answer: Lock across multiple processes using Redis (SETNX, Redlock), ZooKeeper, or database advisory locks. Prevents race conditions.

questions.question: What is lazy loading vs eager loading in data fetching?
questions.answer: Lazy loads data on demand (reduces initial load, may cause N+1). Eager loads all relationships upfront (more data, fewer round trips).

questions.question: What is a write-ahead log (WAL)?
questions.answer: Logging changes before applying to main database, enabling crash recovery and replication (used in PostgreSQL, Cassandra).

questions.question: What is two-phase commit (2PC) vs three-phase commit (3PC)?
questions.answer: 2PC has coordinator single point of failure, blocking. 3PC adds non-blocking timeout (rarely used due complexity).

questions.question: What is vector clock? Use case?
questions.answer: Tracking causality in distributed systems (version vectors). Detects concurrent updates in Dynamo-style databases.

questions.question: What is a gossip protocol? Example?
questions.answer: Peer-to-peer communication where nodes periodically exchange state with random peers. Used in Cassandra, DynamoDB, AWS ECS membership.

questions.question: What is a quorum in distributed systems?
questions.answer: Minimum number of nodes that must agree (e.g., write quorum = W, read quorum = R, with W+R > nodes for strong consistency).

questions.question: What is a Merkle tree? Use case?
questions.answer: Hash tree efficiently verifying data integrity across nodes. Used in Cassandra for anti-entropy repair, Git, blockchain.

questions.question: What is time-series database? Examples.
questions.answer: Optimized for time-stamped data (metrics, IoT). Examples: InfluxDB, Prometheus, TimescaleDB.

questions.question: What is a blob store? Examples.
questions.answer: Object storage for large binaries (images, videos). Examples: Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, Azure Blob.

questions.question: What is a search engine (Elasticsearch, Solr) used for?
questions.answer: Full-text search, log analytics, and near real-time indexing of large datasets. Built on Lucene, distributed.

questions.question: What is a data warehouse vs data lake?
questions.answer: Warehouse: structured, schema-on-write, optimized for analytics (Redshift, BigQuery). Lake: raw unstructured, schema-on-read (S3 + Athena).

questions.question: What is change data capture (CDC)?
questions.answer: Capturing database changes (inserts, updates, deletes) and streaming to other systems (Debezium, Kafka Connect).

questions.question: What is ETL vs ELT?
questions.answer: ETL: extract, transform before loading (warehouse). ELT: extract, load raw, transform inside (data lake, faster for large data).

questions.question: What is an idempotent receiver pattern?
questions.answer: Consumer stores processed message IDs (deduplication) to discard duplicates from retries, ensuring exactly-once semantics.

questions.question: What is outbox pattern in microservices?
questions.answer: Service writes both domain event to outbox table in same transaction; separate publisher reads and sends to message broker, ensuring at-least-once.

questions.question: What is a circuit breaker state (closed, open, half-open)?
questions.answer: Closed: requests pass. Open: requests fail fast (service unhealthy). Half-open: limited test requests after timeout; success closes, failure reopens.

questions.question: What is health check endpoint? Types.
questions.answer: Liveness (is app alive), Readiness (is app ready to accept traffic). Used by load balancers and orchestrators (Kubernetes).

questions.question: What is graceful shutdown vs force kill?
questions.answer: Graceful: stop accepting new requests, finish in-flight, then terminate. Force kill: immediate, may cause data corruption.

questions.question: What is a container (Docker)?
questions.answer: Lightweight, portable unit packaging app and dependencies, isolated using namespaces/cgroups, portable across environments.

questions.question: What is Kubernetes (K8s)?
questions.answer: Orchestration platform automating deployment, scaling, service discovery, and health management of containerized applications.

questions.question: What is a pod in Kubernetes?
questions.answer: Smallest deployable unit containing one or more containers sharing network namespace, storage volumes, and lifecycle.

questions.question: What is a service in Kubernetes? Types.
questions.answer: Abstraction exposing pod network: ClusterIP (internal), NodePort (static port), LoadBalancer (cloud LB), Ingress (L7 HTTP routing).

questions.question: What is a Kubernetes deployment vs statefulset?
questions.answer: Deployment for stateless apps (rolling update, replicas). StatefulSet for stateful (ordered startup, stable network ID, persistent volumes).

questions.question: What is a sidecar container?
questions.answer: Helper container alongside main container (e.g., logging, monitoring, proxy like Envoy) sharing same pod.

questions.question: What is serverless computing (AWS Lambda)?
questions.answer: Executing code in response to events without provisioning servers, auto-scaling, pay-per-invocation. Cold start tradeoff.

questions.question: What is infrastructure as code (IaC)? Tools?
questions.answer: Managing infrastructure via declarative configuration files (Terraform, CloudFormation, Pulumi) enabling version control and automation.

questions.question: What is a content delivery network (CDN) origin shield?
questions.answer: Intermediate layer between origin and edge nodes, caching responses to reduce origin load for popular content.

questions.question: What is an edge computing?
questions.answer: Processing data near source (IoT devices, CDN edge, Lambda@Edge) reducing latency and bandwidth to central cloud.

questions.question: What is a bloom filter implementation tradeoff?
questions.answer: Space efficient (bits array, k hashes). False positives possible, but no false negatives. Choose false positive rate vs size.

questions.question: What is HyperLogLog? Use case?
questions.answer: Probabilistic data structure approximating cardinality (unique count) using constant memory, used for analytics (website unique visitors).

questions.question: What is a count-min sketch? Use case?
questions.answer: Probabilistic hash-based counter for frequency estimation, used in streaming top-k detection, heavy hitters.

questions.question: What is a rate limiter in distributed systems challenge?
questions.answer: Coordinating rate limits across multiple servers requires central store (Redis) or consistent hash sticky sessions.

questions.question: What is a sharding key? How to choose?
questions.answer: Column used to distribute data across shards. Should be high-cardinality, uniformly distributed, and used in most queries.

questions.question: What is a database read replica lag?
questions.answer: Delay between master write and replica apply due to network, hardware, or write load. Can cause stale reads.

questions.question: What is multi-primary replication conflict resolution strategies?
questions.answer: Last-write-wins (timestamp), CRDTs (conflict-free data types), merge (shopping cart), or application-specific (order value).

questions.question: What is a distributed denormalization tradeoff?
questions.answer: Denormalization improves read performance (single query) but increases write complexity, data duplication, and consistency risk.

questions.question: What is a CDN dynamic content acceleration?
questions.answer: Optimizing uncacheable API responses using route optimization, protocol optimization (HTTP/2, TCP/QUIC), edge compute.

questions.question: What is API versioning best practice for long-term support?
questions.answer: URI versioning (/v1/resource), deprecation headers, API evolution with extensible fields, sunset policy (6-12 months overlap).

questions.question: What is a database connection limit? What happens when exceeded?
questions.answer: Maximum concurrent connections (default often ~150). Exceeding causes connection queueing or 'too many connections' errors.

questions.question: What is connection pool starvation? How to diagnose?
questions.answer: Wait time for connection exceeds timeout. Symptoms: high latency, threads blocked. Cause: slow queries, leaks, pool too small.

questions.question: What is a cross-database join? Avoid if possible.
questions.answer: Joining tables across different shards or databases. Avoid by denormalization, application-side joins, or distributed query engines (Trino, Presto).

questions.question: What is data lineage in system design?
questions.answer: Tracking data origin, transformations, and dependencies across pipeline, crucial for debugging, compliance, and impact analysis.

questions.question: What is a deadlock in distributed databases? Resolution?
questions.answer: Transactions waiting for locked resources each other. Detection by wait-for graph, victim selection, abort/retry.

questions.question: What is a distributed ID generator? Requirements?
questions.answer: Unique, roughly increasing, high availability, low latency. Examples: Snowflake (Twitter), UUID v7, leaf segment (MongoDB).

questions.question: What is Snowflake ID format?
questions.answer: 64-bit: timestamp (41 bits) + data center ID (5) + worker ID (5) + sequence (12) = up to 4096 IDs/millisecond per node.

questions.question: What is a UUID/GUID? Pros and cons?
questions.answer: 128-bit universal unique ID. Pros: decentralized generation. Cons:  larger index, random pattern (fragments B-tree). UUID v7 sorts by time.

questions.question: What is database deadlock prevention (lock ordering)?
questions.answer: Always acquiring locks in same global order (e.g., by table name, then row ID) prevents circular wait.

questions.question: What is read-after-write consistency in distributed systems?
questions.answer: Guarantee that a client sees own writes immediately. Hard with replication; solved with read-your-writes consistency, write quorum, sticky sessions.

questions.question: What is monotonic read consistency?
questions.answer: If client reads value X, subsequent reads never see older values (read time moves forward). Prevents data going back in time.

questions.question: What is a causal consistency?
questions.answer: If event A causally precedes B (e.g., comment after post), all nodes see A before B. Weaker than strong, uses vector clocks.

questions.question: What is a conflict-free replicated data type (CRDT)?
questions.answer: Data structure that converges automatically across replicas without coordination (e.g., G-Counter, OR-Set). Used in automerge, Redis.

questions.question: What is a log-structured merge-tree (LSM tree)? Why used?
questions.answer: Storage engine: memtable (writes) + SSTables (immutable sorted). High write throughput (Cassandra, RocksDB, LevelDB).

questions.question: What is a B-tree vs LSM tree tradeoff?
questions.answer: B-tree: faster reads, no write amplification, but random writes. LSM: faster writes, compression, but read amplification, compaction overhead.

questions.question: What is a vector timestamp vs Lamport clock?
questions.answer: Lamport clock provides logical time but not causality (concurrent events indistinguishable). Vector clock tracks causality via per-node counters.

questions.question: What is a Byzantine fault tolerance (BFT)?
questions.answer: System tolerating arbitrary/malicious failures (not just crash). Used in blockchain (PBFT, Tendermint). Requires 3f+1 nodes.
